context: June 17, 2026 (Tuesday) — Father's Day passed June 15 (2 days ago). Summer week 3 for US families. July 4th Independence Day in 17 days. Back-to-school in ~7 weeks (early August some states, late August most). Baptism season ongoing through September. Last morning factory run was June 6 — 11-day gap. Article written today: 'Why Does My Child Lie?' (from June 6 pending seed backlog).

seeds.title: Why Your Child Acts Out More With You Than With Anyone Else
target_keywords: why does my child act out with me
child behaves for everyone except me
child acts out at home not school
seeds.hook: The teacher says he's a dream. The grandparents say he's an angel. Then he walks in the door and it's forty-five minutes of meltdown. I used to think I was doing something wrong. Turns out I was doing something right.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Children fall apart with their safest person. The meltdown that happens with you and not the teacher is not a parenting failure — it's the evidence of your attachment. The teacher gets the composed version because the child is protecting that relationship. You get the real one because they trust you won't leave. This reframe is completely counterintuitive and deeply reassuring. It names an invisible win that parents mistake for a problem every single day.
seeds.seo_notes: One of the most searched parenting frustrations — appears in forums, parenting subs, pediatric advice sites constantly. Phrase 'behaves for everyone except me' gets significant volume. AlreadyLoved answer is the counter-intuitive reframe: it's a trust signal, not a discipline failure. High evergreen value, strong share-and-save potential.

seeds.title: What Is Secure Attachment? (And How Do You Know If Your Child Has It?)
target_keywords: signs of secure attachment in children
how to build secure attachment with child
seeds.hook: You've probably heard the phrase 'secure attachment' in parenting content. But what does it actually look like on a Tuesday afternoon? Not in a study. In your house. With your kid.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Secure attachment is not about never being angry, never missing bedtime, never saying the wrong thing. It's about repair. About the child knowing — in their body, not just their head — that the relationship comes back. The attached child cries harder because they trust you can hold it. They misbehave more freely because they trust you won't leave. They say 'I hate you' because they know you'll still be there tomorrow. Secure attachment is the invisible infrastructure of every piece of content AlreadyLoved creates. This is the definitional piece.
seeds.seo_notes: High AI citation potential — direct definitional question. Featured snippet territory. Growing search volume as 'secure attachment' becomes mainstream parenting vocabulary. Strong link to AlreadyLoved's core philosophy. Article should open with a direct, quotable definition in the first paragraph.

seeds.title: The Ordinary Things That Tell Your Child They Belong
target_keywords: small ways to show kids they belong
seeds.hook: It is never the big thing. It is the Thursday afternoon. The way you look up from your phone when she comes in the room. The way you say her name when you're calling her for dinner — like it still means something to you that it's her specifically.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Children don't build their sense of belonging from the grand gestures — the birthdays, the trips, the bedtime I love yous spoken on autopilot. They build it from the accumulated evidence of the ordinary Thursday. The look-up. The interest that doesn't need them to perform anything to hold it. The way you say their name. This piece catalogs those small, uncounted moments — and the research that shows why they matter more than anything dramatic. Deeply relatable. High save/share. Brand-defining.
seeds.seo_notes: Lower search volume but extremely high Pinterest save rate. These 'small ways to show love' pieces perform well organically through social. Natural brand-building — every item on the list is something AlreadyLoved books reinforce. Good summer timing — parents looking for connection over vacation and slow days.

seeds.title: What the Bored Kid Is Actually Saying
target_keywords: what to do with bored kids
seeds.hook: 'I'm bored.' She said it at 9:15 in the morning. We'd been awake for forty minutes. And instead of immediately reaching for an activity, I sat down on the floor and thought: what is she actually asking for?
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Boredom in children is often identity looking for itself. When the schedule disappears — as it does in summer — children have to discover who they are when they're not performing a role. That process feels uncomfortable, and they name the discomfort as boredom. But parents who tolerate the boredom (rather than immediately solving it) are giving their child something rare: the space to find out what they actually want, not what they've been told to want. This is the upstream reason unstructured time matters — not just creativity or independence, but identity formation. Perfect summer timing. High engagement piece.
seeds.seo_notes: Peak search volume in June-August. High relatable-engagement value — universal summer parenting moment. AlreadyLoved angle (boredom as identity-searching, not a problem to solve) is genuinely distinctive from typical 'activities for bored kids' listicles. Will drive comments and shares.

seeds.title: What We're Really Celebrating on the Fourth of July
target_keywords: what to teach kids about independence day
seeds.hook: There's the kind of freedom that's about a country. And then there's the kind that lives in a child — the freedom to try and fail and be loved anyway. To belong before they've proven anything. That second kind is what we're actually trying to give them.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Freedom isn't taught through parades. It's built inside a child through ten thousand moments of unconditional belonging. A child who knows their place in the family isn't contingent on their performance is free in a way that has nothing to do with history or geography — they are free from the exhausting work of earning love. They can explore, fail, disagree, and find out who they are. That's the real independence we want for them. July 4 is a good day to talk about it.
seeds.urgency: July 4 in 17 days — safe publish deadline ~June 26. Article should be written next run.
seeds.seo_notes: Moderate search volume around July 4 parenting content. Strong Pinterest/IG performance for values-based holiday pieces. AlreadyLoved angle connects freedom to identity and belonging — completely distinct from civics-lesson content. Good brand-building piece.

seeds.title: Personalized Baptism Gifts That Will Last Longer Than the Day
seeds.hook: The candles are out. The white dress is dry-cleaned and boxed. The cake is gone. What's left on the shelf? If you're looking for a baptism gift that stays, you're asking the right question.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: A baptism marks something: this child is known, this child is named, this child belongs to something larger than themselves. The best gifts for that day carry that same message — not just something decorative, but something that holds the meaning. A book with their name in it, woven into a story about who they are from the start. That's the category of gift this piece makes the case for — meaningful over decorative. Strong commercial alignment.
seeds.seo_notes: Baptism season runs May-September (peak June-August). High commercial intent — people searching this are ready to buy. Different from the May 29 article about baptism ceremony itself — this is strictly gift-focused. Good Pinterest performance for gift-guide format.

seeds.title: Back to School Isn't a To-Do List. It's a Transition.
target_keywords: helping kids transition back to school
seeds.hook: Every August I start writing lists. Backpacks. Supplies. Lunch ideas. New shoes. And every August my daughter does the same thing — starts getting quieter around week three. Getting more clingy. Having more meltdowns at bedtime. The lists don't touch that.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Back-to-school content is almost entirely logistical. What children actually need is someone who sees the emotional reality of the transition — that going back to school means their sense of who-they-are gets tested all over again. New room, new teacher, new social hierarchy. Their belonging is suddenly uncertain. The things that help are not the fresh folder and the new pencils. They're the moments of connection before the bus comes. The reminder that their place at home hasn't changed while their place at school is still settling. This piece addresses the part everyone skips.
seeds.urgency: Back-to-school content peaks mid-July through August. Safe publish deadline July 15 — write next 3 weeks.
seeds.seo_notes: High-volume search window July-August. Strong Pinterest performance. AlreadyLoved angle on belonging and identity transition is genuinely distinctive — no competitor is writing about back-to-school as an identity event. Natural personalized book CTA (book with their name helps them feel known going in).
